run.lesson_text: Teach equivalent fractions using equal circles.
run.lesson_spec_json: {"goal":"Students will use equal-sized circle models to recognize, generate, and explain equivalent fractions by showing that different fraction names can represent the same area of one whole.","successCriteria":["Partition equal-sized circles into equal parts.","Shade circle models to represent given fractions.","Compare shaded areas only when the circles represent equal-sized wholes.","Identify equivalent fractions when their shaded areas cover the same amount of equal-sized circles.","Generate a simple equivalent fraction by subdividing every part of a circle in the same way.","Explain equivalence using a model and a statement such as “1/2 equals 2/4 because both shade the same amount of an equal-sized whole.”"],"prerequisiteKnowledge":["A fraction describes equal parts of one whole.","The denominator tells how many equal parts make the whole.","The numerator tells how many equal parts are being considered.","A circle can represent one whole when its boundary and size remain fixed.","Equal parts must have equal area, even if their shapes do not look identical."],"misconceptions":[{"signal":"The student compares fractions modeled with circles of different sizes and decides equivalence from the visible shaded size alone.","response":"Provide equal-sized circle templates and restate that equivalent fractions must refer to the same-sized whole. Have the student overlay or align the circles before comparing."},{"signal":"The student treats any collection of slices as fractional parts, even when the parts are unequal.","response":"Ask whether each part has the same area. Repartition the circle with clear diameters or fold lines, then identify how many equal parts make the whole."},{"signal":"The student says 2/4 is greater than 1/2 because 2 is greater than 1.","response":"Place equal-sized models of 1/2 and 2/4 side by side or overlay them. Focus attention on the total shaded area rather than the number of shaded pieces."},{"signal":"The student changes only the numerator or only the denominator when generating an equivalent fraction.","response":"Demonstrate subdividing every existing section equally. Connect the visual change to multiplying both numerator and denominator by the same whole number."},{"signal":"The student counts partition lines instead of counting equal regions.","response":"Trace and count the enclosed regions one at a time, then use that count as the denominator."},{"signal":"The student assumes fractions are equivalent because they have the same numerator or denominator.","response":"Have the student shade both fractions on equal-sized circles and compare the covered area, then state equivalence or non-equivalence from the models."}],"checkpoints":[{"id":"partition-and-represent","skill":"Represent a fraction with an equal-circle model.","evidenceOfMastery":"Given three equal-sized blank circles and fractions such as 1/2, 2/3, and 3/4, the student partitions each circle into the correct number of equal-area parts and shades the correct number."},{"id":"match-equivalent-models","skill":"Recognize equivalent fractions from visual models.","evidenceOfMastery":"From a set of equal-sized circle models, the student correctly matches at least three equivalent pairs, including 1/2 and 2/4, and explains that each pair covers the same amount of the whole."},{"id":"generate-by-subdivision","skill":"Generate an equivalent fraction by subdividing a circle.","evidenceOfMastery":"Starting with a shaded model such as 1/2, the student divides every part in the same way to create 2/4 or 3/6 and records a correct equality statement."},{"id":"distinguish-nonexamples","skill":"Determine when two modeled fractions are not equivalent.","evidenceOfMastery":"Given equivalent and non-equivalent pairs on equal-sized circles, the student classifies at least four of five pairs correctly and cites unequal shaded area for each nonexample."},{"id":"explain-equivalence","skill":"Justify fraction equivalence with mathematical language and a circle model.","evidenceOfMastery":"The student completes an oral or written explanation: “___/___ is equivalent to ___/___ because the equal-sized circles show the same shaded area,” while accurately identifying numerators, denominators, and equal parts."}],"profiles":[{"id":"short-concrete-loops","label":"Pineapple","supports":["Use brief cycles of model, shade, compare, and explain.","Present one fraction pair at a time with immediate feedback.","Use movable circle pieces or foldable paper circles before recording equations.","Include a quick mastery check after each cycle."],"constraints":[]},{"id":"audio-first","label":"Blueberry","supports":["State directions and fraction comparisons aloud before showing written notation.","Offer oral descriptions of models and allow spoken explanations of equivalence.","Use a consistent verbal frame: “Both circles are the same size; both have the same shaded area.”","Pair spoken numerator and denominator language with pointing to the corresponding model parts."],"constraints":[]},{"id":"math-language-support","label":"Mango","supports":["Begin with a worked example showing 1/2 becoming 2/4 through equal subdivision.","Annotate the worked example with “equal whole,” “equal parts,” “numerator,” “denominator,” and “same shaded area.”","Provide sentence frames for comparisons and justifications.","Keep a completed example visible while the student solves a parallel problem."],"constraints":[]}]}

{"browser":{"passed":true,"consoleErrors":[],"outboundRequests":[],"screenshotPath":"/Users/alanany/Desktop/LessonBend/artifacts/33472571-654c-4d2a-bdbd-0a0f23d4c7b8.png"},"evaluation":{"passes":true,"reasons":["The artifact targets the specified goal through equal-sized circle models and includes five short mastery loops aligned to the named checkpoints: representing, matching, subdividing, distinguishing nonexamples, and explaining equivalence.","It supports the Pineapple profile with brief model–shade–compare–explain cycles, one focused task at a time, immediate feedback, and progress/mastery tracking.","Correct feedback visibly combines a green panel, check icon, explicit “Correct!” text, and a small celebration animation.","Retry feedback visibly combines a warm amber panel, retry icon, encouraging retry text, explicit next-move guidance supplied through the cue, and a gentle animated arrow/highlight. This satisfies the feedback intent despite the static checker’s false negative.","Colour is not the sole feedback channel: icons, text, borders, patterns, and model labels provide redundant cues. Feedback is also announced through an aria-live region, and reduced-motion preferences are respected.","The worked 1/2 = 2/4 example emphasizes equal-sized wholes, equal subdivision, and equal shaded area, directly addressing central misconceptions.","The implementation appears safe: browser checks passed, there were no console errors or outbound requests, and no unsafe external content is used."],"repairInstructions":[]}}

{"browser":{"passed":true,"consoleErrors":[],"outboundRequests":[],"screenshotPath":"/Users/alanany/Desktop/LessonBend/artifacts/5fc43b67-be5d-4dc7-ad22-165bbffe57ef.png"},"evaluation":{"passes":false,"reasons":["The lesson goal and visible checkpoint structure align with recognizing and generating equivalent fractions using equal-sized circle models, and the design includes Mango-oriented worked-example and math-language supports.","The required feedback rule is not met. Colour or styling infrastructure alone does not satisfy the requirement.","The artifact appears safe in the browser checks: there are no console errors or outbound requests.","The missing validation hooks are not, by themselves, an instructional or accessibility failure because no particular DOM structure is required; however, the visible multichannel feedback failures require repair."],"repairInstructions":["For every correct checkpoint response, render a visible green feedback panel containing an affirmative icon such as ✓, explicit text such as “Correct” or “Success,” and the existing small celebration. Ensure the icon is accompanied by text rather than relying on colour or animation.","For every incorrect or incomplete response, render a visible warm amber panel containing a retry icon such as ↻, explicit retry wording, and a specific next move—for example, “Try again: compare the total shaded areas of the equal-sized circles.” Include a gentle visual cue and respect reduced-motion preferences.","Apply these feedback combinations consistently to every scored checkpoint, not only final lesson completion.","Verify that each Check button genuinely scores the required evidence of mastery and only marks a checkpoint mastered when its full threshold is met. Preserve student choices after a retry so the learner can revise them.","Optionally add `data-lb-feedback=\"success\"` and `data-lb-feedback=\"retry\"` to the corresponding rendered feedback containers so automated validation can identify them, while retaining the visible instructional features above."]}}
